speaker: interviewer
text: --the Interview with Erna Florsheim, October 20, 1996. You were telling me about where Saint-Jean is in France and that, at a certain point, it became wise to take you to a convent. Could you tell me what the date was at that time, or approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Could you tell me the date that you left Saint-Jean to go to Toulon?

speaker: interviewee
text: November 1, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long do you think you stayed in the convent?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few weeks. It was not very long, because by that time the Germans moved on, and it was towards the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know it was towards the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the people that Margot worked for wanted to go back to Toulon, that they left before. And they told us that it was close to the end of the war, and so I decided to go with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were in the convent, and did you choose to act like a Catholic out of respect, or did you-- was it necessary that you do that in order to hide your identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did it because I was asked to do it. I don't know for sure if they tried to convert me. Maybe they would have liked to try in case I would have had to stay there, that no one would have come for me. I do not-- that I cannot say for certain. I did it because-- only because I was asked to do, to kneel with them, and they taught me the prayers. And I-- I went along with whatever they wanted me to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they knew you were Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they did know, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they know your actual name, or did they know you as Elise?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did it happen that you went to Toulon? Did you travel together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Dr. and Madame Guillaude, they had a truck where they took their belongings with them. And Margot and I were on the truck with them, traveling with them at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel more relaxed traveling together with a friend knowing--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything that made you feel good that you remember, or anything that you saw that you remember, or the way the air felt?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- in a way, it was a relief to leave that small town. There was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the closeness of the people. They were very close, it seems, and there was always a lot of gossiping, and I felt very uncomfortable. And one day, a young girl that lived next-- in the next house where I was working said to me, you know, all you are is a maid. And I-- I knew-- of course, I knew it, but it hurt just the same. And so the other people-- I had no contact with anyone really, except the people where Margot worked for and Amelie worked for, and these were educated people. And they treated me-- us, I should say-- with respect, although we worked very hard and, believe me, from early morning until late at night. And-- but we had a roof over our heads, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so we were grateful for that. But I was very relieved to leave there, and I was always happy that I made the decision to leave. It's-- I had to make so many decisions in my life that, from very young on, that it's-- it was not difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you miss the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the-- at first, because they were very lovely, especially the little baby. She was adorable. The older girl who was a little bit older already, she was, how shall I say-- from that young girl that hurt me with words talked to that-- the oldest to hurt me. And I-- I never answered. Whatever that little girl said, I just let it go, and I never answered. But she-- if we-- she poisoned that little girl. But she was a nice girl, and the people, the parents, I never told the parents about it, of course. I-- you know. But I heard later on that the parents separated, and I don't know what happened to the children. I don't know where they went.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you went to Toulon, did you ever have contact with that family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. As I said, they separated, the couple, and probably divorced later on. I never heard from them any more. And this is-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about Toulon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Toulon is very pretty. And it was-- it's-- the only thing about Toulon is they have a wind, which is called mistral. And it was very forceful at times, but the sun was always shining. And I worked for Dr. Guillaud. And her father, Mrs. Guillaud's father, live-- lived in Toulon, and he was there when we came there. He ran a hotel. As-- as a matter of fact, the Germans had just left. They had taken over the whole hotel. And his name was Mr. [FRENCH], and he was-- he knew we were coming, and he was very nice. Margot worked in the house, took care of the child and the housework, and I did the-- the hotel. And I-- they had a housekeeper which was very strict. I had to do all the sheets, everything. I had to carry the heavy baskets, wet laundry, and I had to bring it to a certain place, and it had to be boiled, everything. And-- and then I had to iron it, and if the crease was not just so, I had to do the whole thing over again. And I was-- I worked-- it's unbelievable how much work I had to do. But then, again, I had a roof over my head, and I had some food. And it's-- it was difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you eat the hotel food?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't have hotel food. I ate whatever Margot cooked. What we-- Margot and I, we ate together in the kitchen, and they ate separately. And I-- it wasn't much, whatever they could get, you know. Also in those days, there was not much available. So-- but that was also at-- at that time, as long as we had something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you paid for this work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They-- as Margot said to me not so long ago, they had very cheap help. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you remain in Toulon at this hotel? What was the hotel's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hotel De La Mer is the name, was the name. And as a matter of fact, Mrs. Guillaud's father passed away some years ago, but she still owns the hotel. When we were in France two years ago, I contacted them, because in the wintertime they live in Nice where their children live. So I only spoke to them on the phone, and she wanted me to come to Toulon to the hotel. But we were with a group, and we didn't have the time. She still opens the hotel for guests. But-- but I must go back to when I first came to Toulon. And then the American soldiers came, where they are stationed there, and then they took over the hotel. I don't know if they paid or so. I don't know. But very often when I was cleaning the rooms, they used to give me a bar of chocolate or something. When the housekeeper, she used to come around, and I had a little basket with all the cleaning things in there, she looked in there, and she found it and she took it. So-- so I remember that. And she was really something.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, did you feel that things would work out--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you had--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had more hope, because I-- I knew eventually it will change.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have contact with anyone from the outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In the-- at that time, the war was not over, and it was-- it was getting towards the end. But we could not-- I could not write to England. I didn't even know where my sister or my brother, where they were. And no, I had no contact.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I know you were working very hard. Was there any time to read or to do anything else or to visit with Margot, even if-- you know, not just to sit and eat, but to do something else?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we were so tired at night. We'd just be-- we were exhausted. We didn't do anything. We didn't go any place. Once a week, they gave us a few hours off on a Sunday afternoon, and Margot and I-- money we didn't have. We went-- we walked for a little bit and to see the surrounding, and it was really very pretty there. But we couldn't really enjoy anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there in Toulon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until I went to Marseille April 16, 1945. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to Marseille because I wanted to be in a bigger city where I could arrange to go eventually to my sister and brothers in England. So the OSE, again, I contacted them, and they said-- sent me to Marseille, also, again, to a family with children, and I worked there. And they-- there was another lady there, also a refugee, and she took care of the child, and I did the other work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Margot remain in Toulon?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- no, she left and went to Paris. She had an uncle there who returned to-- to Paris with his wife and son and daughter. And she went to them, and she stayed with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you leave about the same time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Margot left a little before I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the conditions like in Marseille?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Marseille it was the same, more or less, as in Toulon. I worked for the people, and I did not get any pay. And I-- again, for the food, what they gave me, and-- which was very-- I remember especially there, they make a little toast, and then they made a white sauce over it. And they gave me that almost every day, and I ate it. I was hungry. I was young, and so I ate it. But I never-- I never want to see that-- that-- anything that looks like it, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you remain there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Marseille? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Until-- not very long, until June 18, 1945. And I went to Paris, and there I also-- I worked for a couple. Letherman was the name. And they lived-- they had a house, and they had a little baby. I took-- I didn't take care of the baby. The mother herself took care of the baby. But I took care of the house and everything else. They were very nice to me. Her husband was a lawyer. And as a matter of fact, two years ago, I was-- I looked up the address, but it was during the summer, and everybody was-- is on a vacation in August in Paris. I could not reach them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you made this arrangement yourself to go to this family in Paris, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --were you assisted by OSE also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also. OSE took care of everything. And in Paris, I stayed with Mr. and Mrs. Letherman until I went to England. And that was-- I went to England [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] August 21, 1945. So I was only from June to August in--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to go back just a little bit. Where were you at the time of liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Marseille.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to Marseille, then you went north, and then you came back south again. What brought you to Marseille the second time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was there-- I was only there once.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, and then you went to Paris afterwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went to Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: I beg your pardon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you describe what liberation was like in Marseille?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- the streets were crowded with people and the soldiers, the Americans. And it was unbelievable, the-- the joy that they were-- the people were free. And it was-- they-- they-- when-- I was walking beside the other lady that I worked with in that same home in the street. And I-- we were walking along, and somebody grabbed me and kissed me. So she said to me, if you don't want to be kissed, just hold onto me. So I remember it was such a jubilation. It was indescribable, yes. And then I knew-- I knew that eventually I would be able to go to England, which I did. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And of course I was searching for my parents. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I wrote to Konigsbach, if they heard anything. And I wrote to the International Red Cross and if they heard anything, if my parents were liberated from somewhere [INAUDIBLE]. But of course not.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you find out from them, from the International Red Cross?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only that they were contacting the-- the Red Cross in Paris, and I will hear from them. But I heard from them eventually where they said that they were-- my parents were shipped towards Auschwitz, and they did not return.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you find your brothers and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was in-- when I was in England, in London, there's a Jewish-- was a Jewish organization, and it was called the Bloomsbury House, and it was on Bloomsbury Street. I-- I suppose that's why the-- the name. And I-- don't remember. It must have been the OSE that also contacted them that I-- that I would like to go to England where my sister and my brothers were. And so they assisted me then-- from then on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, at that point, when the tide of your life was changing, what hopes and dreams did you have for yourself and the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I-- I remember so well. I made up my mind I'm not going to clean the floors for the rest of my life. I'm going to learn something, whatever it is. I wanted to study whatever. And since-- since childhood, I always wanted to go into nursing. I told that to the people in London, if there's a possibility that I could go into training for that profession. They-- but they said, it will take a long time. And first of all, I had to finish high school, which I had not finished-- I-- which I did later on. And then I went into training, but there I made a mistake. I went-- took that course for newborn care and nursery school teaching. What I should have done-- even so, it took an additional year to take as a registered nurse, to train for that. But I had no money. I had nothing to wear practically. I had to make a living for myself. So I want to do something the fastest way that I could. So I regret that [SIGHS] move very much that I made at that time, but I had really not much choice. But I did go into training and completed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the schooling paid for?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- I-- I'm not sure if the Bloomsbury House arranged something, or because it was from the Church of England. I don't know if it was free or not. I-- I'm not sure about that at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your living expenses? How did you manage?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I lived in the-- in that place where I trained. And then when-- on my day off, I was-- went to my youngest brother, Sigi He was in a hostel. He grew up there. And I stayed with them. And the Bloomsbury House-- I mean, also that organization paid for my brother, all these young people that were there, and I assume they paid for me also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe to me how you found your brothers and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I also-- I wrote-- I told the people in-- before I went to England, I-- I wrote also to the Red Cross, I remember. And they got in touch with the Bloomsbury House in London, and they found-- they found out where my sister was and my-- both my brothers. My sister and older brother were interned on the Isle of Man for a while in England because they were aliens, and-- but later on they were released. Both-- my sister went to the British Army, the ATS, and my brother was in the army also for a while. And I don't recall if they became British citizens or not. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But my youngest brother grew up really in Ilkley in Yorkshire. That is-- the next city is Leeds. And I trained in-- nearby. It was called-- what's it called? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was called Birley in Yorkshire, [BACKROUND NOISE] which is very close to Leeds. And during the war years, did your brothers and sister maintain contact with one another?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes. Yes, they did. And they used to visit my youngest brother in that hostel because-- and they both worked. My sister worked as a domestic. My brother Julius, he worked as a waiter to make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Taking one last look back over those years before you were reunited with your family, does any incident of inspiring goodness stand out, or any incident at all, incidents at all, that-- that touched you one way or another that you'd like to tell?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't think of anything in particular. When you're very young, it's very hard. And if you work hard, and you-- you don't look-- you look more on the negative side than the positive side. I was grateful that the people did not mistreat me, even though I worked hard. I was fortunate in that respect. And-- but other than that, nothing really stands out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you actually become a nurse?

speaker: interviewee
text: I became a nurse-- I graduated, it was in 1948, [SIGHS] January 1948. I took my exams in London. They were two-days exams, one day oral and one day written.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what made you decide to come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I got my diploma, I-- my uncle came to the United States in the meantime. He was-- from Belgium to Canada, then to the United States. And I wrote to him that I was happy that I graduated, and I passed my exams. And he said to me, why don't you come to America? What's the difference where you work, in England or here? And here you have some family? My sister and my-- both my brothers wanted to come here to America. And so-- but in the meantime, my sister went to South America to live. She got married there. She lived there a number of years. But both my brothers wanted to come here. So I said to them, I have a chance to go to America. Let me go, and I will see to it that you get the papers to come to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came here in August 1948, [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But before I came to London even, I was in Cheam in Surrey. That was the last part of my training. But that doesn't-- it's not so-- so important. But I-- August 10, 1948, I came to New York. Did you speak any English? I know you were in London for a while but--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- my English was quite good when I came to England, because I had private lessons in Germany, and I also had Spanish private lessons. But English, I-- although I could not speak it, after a few months in England my ear got used to the sound, and I didn't have a problem. And of course, I went to first to school to finish high school. And then I-- it helped me when I was in training that I studied hard late at night, under the covers I used to study because the light had to be out at a certain time. But I-- I wanted to-- I didn't want to fail my exam. So I-- but English, it came fairly easy to me because I had a good foundation.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came here by boat and the Nieuw-- with the and I arrived in-- I believe it was in Hoboken. And my cousin, my uncle, they were there to greet me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if anyone on the boat was also coming from a similar situation? Did you make friends with anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't speak to anyone. I was-- I was shy, overwhelmed. I didn't know what the future will hold. I was worried, although I had some family here. But still, I was, by that time, at an age where I have to, you know, I had to take care of myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to ask you one last question with regard to those times. Were you able to observe, outside of your own heart, any of your religious feelings? Were you aware of when the holidays were, and were you able to, in any way, observe them during the war years?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't observe them, but I knew approximately when Yom Kippur, Rosh Hashanah should be. Sometimes I fasted on a day. I had no idea was it the day or not. I felt it must have been about that time. But we were deported. It was the last days of Sukkot. And so I knew before that was Yom Kippur, you know, a week before and-- and Rosh Hashanah, so approximately. No, I never knew until I came to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came to England did you-- did you fall back into your religious observance spontaneously?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was always with me. I never lost it completely. And I-- how shall I say, I could not observe the way I was taught at home because I was in training, and I was the only Jewish girl there. My brother [? Siggy ?] was in a Jewish-- in that home that was all Jewish. They were not very observant. They did observe some of the holidays and-- but it was not-- it's only-- actually, I would say, I observe properly again when I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.
